source_label: Greg Maxwell — CoinJoin: Bitcoin privacy for the real world (2013)
excerpt: Bitcoin transactions can have multiple inputs and outputs. The CoinJoin idea is that several users agree on a transaction that combines their inputs, paying their respective outputs, and each user signs only after verifying their own output is present. No user ever surrenders custody, and the coordinator cannot steal because every signature only authorizes the all-or-nothing transaction.
body:
Source excerpt:
Bitcoin transactions can have multiple inputs and outputs. The CoinJoin idea is that several users agree on a transaction that combines their inputs, paying their respective outputs, and each user signs only after verifying their own output is present. No user ever surrenders custody, and the coordinator cannot steal because every signature only authorizes the all-or-nothing transaction.

---

Cited by leaf "Coinjoin":
A coinjoin is a single Bitcoin transaction that combines inputs from many independent participants and pays out equal-sized outputs to each of them, so an outside observer can't tell which input funded which output [1]. The construction is a 2013 idea from Greg Maxwell: no participant ever signs a transaction that takes their funds away from their own keys, and the coordinator who builds the transaction can be untrusted because every input is signed only after the full draft is verified.

In practice, the most-used designs are JoinMarket — a market where makers earn small fees for offering their coins as decoys to takers — and the WabiSabi family, which uses Chaumian credentials so the coordinator can't link a participant's input to their output even if it tries to [2]. Output uniformity is the core privacy property; if your output is one of fifty 0.01 BTC outputs in the same transaction, the chain says any of fifty users could be your recipient.

What coinjoin doesn't do: it doesn't unlink coins from their pre-coinjoin history (a chain analyst can still see the inputs went into a coinjoin), it doesn't help if you spend a post-mix output back to a KYC venue under your own name, and it doesn't protect against coordinators that log IP addresses or against later post-mix "toxic change" combining [3]. It is a tool that provides a measurable anonymity set for one transaction, not a magic eraser. Use of well-known coinjoin scripts is also publicly identifiable — that fact alone is sometimes enough to get an exchange account flagged, depending on jurisdiction.
